[{"title": "Total Addressable Market (TAM): The Complete Guide to Market Sizing for ...", "url": "https://www.businessinitiative.org/business-tips/total-addressable-market/", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Master Total AddressableMarketanalysis to unlock growth opportunities, secure funding, and make strategic decisions that drive business success.", "relevance_score": 0.6}, {"title": "Total Addressable Market - What Is TAM & How to Calculate It | Toptal\u00ae", "url": "https://www.toptal.com/management-consultants/market-sizing/total-addressable-market-example", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Total addressablemarket(TAM, occasionally referred to as total availablemarket)isa form ofmarketsizing that enables a business to define the holistic revenue opportunity offered from its product or service.", "relevance_score": 0.6}, {"title": "What Is Total Addressable Market (TAM) and Why It Matters - SMARTe", "url": "https://www.smarte.pro/blog/total-addressable-market-tam", "snippet": "[DDG Snippet] Learn what Total AddressableMarket(TAM)isand why it matters for understandingmarketsize, growth potential, and opportunity.", "relevance_score": 0.6}, {"title": "Total addressable market - Wikipedia", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market", "snippet": "[Jina Parallel Content] Title: Total addressable market URL Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market Published Time: 2008-08-01T22:17:56Z Markdown Content: From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia [![Image 1](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/TAM-SAM-Market.jpg/330px-TAM-SAM-Market.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:TAM-SAM-Market.jpg) An [Euler diagram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euler_diagram \"Euler diagram\") showing the relationship between total available market (TAM), [served (or serviceable) available market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serviceable_available_market \"Serviceable available market\"), and [target market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Target_market \"Target market\") **Total addressable market** (**TAM**), also called **total available market**, is a term that is typically used to reference the [revenue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Revenue \"Revenue\") opportunity for a product or service. TAM helps prioritize [business opportunities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business_opportunity \"Business opportunity\") by serving as a size [metric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Performance_indicator \"Performance indicator\") of a given opportunity's underlying potential.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market#cite_note-The-Startup-Owners-Manual-2012-1) TAM can be defined as a global total (even if a particular company could not reach some of it) or, more commonly, a sub-market that one specific product or service could serve (within realistic expansion scenarios). The inclusion of constraints such as distribution and competition challenges then modifies the concept, reducing the market down to the [serviceable available market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serviceable_available_market \"Serviceable available market\") (SAM), the percentage of the market that can be served (either by that company or all providers) out of the TAM.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market#cite_note-The-Startup-Owners-Manual-2012-1) This is occasionally referred to as PAU (Potential Active Use). Competitive dynamics reduce the SAM to SOM. ## Difference between TAM, SAM and SOM [[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Total_addressable_market&action=edit&section=1 \"Edit section: Difference between TAM, SAM and SOM\")] Total addressable market (TAM), or total available market, is the total market demand for a product or service,[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market#cite_note-:0-2) calculated in annual revenue or unit sales if 100% of the available market is achieved. Serviceable available market (SAM) is the portion of TAM that is reachable by a company's distribution footprint.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_addressable_market#cite_note-:0-2) The SAM may be the same as the [Target Market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Target_Market \"Target Market\"), but a Target Market can also be a subset of the SAM. For example, a beverage company may be able to reach a", "relevance_score": 0.85}, {"title": "Statista - The Statistics Portal for Market Data, Market Research and ...", "url": worldwide](https://www.statista.com/statistics/241683/nikes-sales-worldwide-since-2004/)\n...\n*   [Artificial intelligence (AI)", "relevance_score": 0.85}, {"title": "Total Addressable Market (TAM) | Formula + Calculator", "url": "https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/total-addressable-market-tam/", "snippet": "[Jina Parallel Content] In simple terms, the total addressable market (TAM) reflects the revenue that a company could theoretically generate, assuming a 100% [market share](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/market-share/). For companies of all sizes\u2014ranging from early-stage startups to established, low-growth companies\u2014market sizing is an essential step in determining the [growth](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/growth-rate/) potential of the company. *   **Early-Stage \u279d** Startups estimate the [market size](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/market-sizing/) to see if a market, and the implied revenue opportunity, is worth undertaking the risk. *   **Growth-Stage \u279d** Growth companies track the market size to measure their progress at capturing market share and to estimate the remaining upside potential that can be attained. *   **Late-Stage \u279d** Mature companies are attentive to the market size to decide if the current revenue is sustainable or if a strategic pivot is necessary via market expansion (i.e. introduce new products or services, expand into international markets). The total addressable market (TAM) of a company can be expressed in terms of the total number of potential customers, split out by customer types (e.g. SMBs, large enterprises). However, the far more prevalent method in practice is for the TAM to be measured in terms of revenue rather than potential customers. The most basic method to estimate the total addressable market (TAM) is the top-down method. The top-down approach provides a broad estimate by multiplying the [average revenue per user (ARPU)](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/arpu-average-revenue-per-user/) by the total number of potential customers. **Total Addressable Market (TAM) =**[Average Revenue per User](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/arpu-average-revenue-per-user/) (ARPU) **\u00d7** Total Number of Potential Customers Of course, the market size must be further analyzed and distilled to arrive at a more accurate figure. But as a \u201cback-of-the-envelope\u201d method, the formula should suffice. The average revenue per user (ARPU), on the other hand, is an assumption that should be based in accordance with historical precedence and market data on comparable products (or adjacent offerings). In short, the ARPU must reflect the [pricing](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/pricing-power/) that customers should be willing to pay, which should be a function of the value delivered to the customer. Conversely, the revenue metric can be switched with the annual average contract value ([ACV](https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/annual-contract-value-acv/)), which pertains to the SaaS industry and subscription-based businesses. **Total Addressable Market (TAM) =** Annual Contract Value (ACV) **\u00d7** Total Number of Potential Accounts If a startup seeks to acquire 10% of a market sized at a $1 billion TAM, that implies a target revenue of approximately $100 million. *   Implied Revenue = 10% Take Rate \u00d7 $1 billio", "relevance_score": 0.85}]
